title: Contact Statistics & Reports
description: Use comprehensions and iteration tools to generate statistics and reports from your contact data. Create contact_stats() that computes summary data, and group_by_domain() that groups contacts by their email domain.
starter_code:
# Contact Manager — Step 10: Statistics & Reports
# Use comprehensions and iteration to analyze contact data.

contacts = [
    {'name': 'Alice', 'phone': '555-0001', 'email': 'alice@example.com'},
    {'name': 'Bob', 'phone': '555-0002', 'email': 'bob@example.com'},
    {'name': 'Charlie', 'phone': '555-0003', 'email': 'charlie@work.org'},
    {'name': 'Diana', 'phone': '555-0004', 'email': 'diana@work.org'},
    {'name': 'Eve', 'phone': '555-0005', 'email': 'eve@example.com'},
]

def contact_stats(contact_list):
    """Return a dict with statistics about the contacts:
    {
        'total': number of contacts,
        'domains': number of unique email domains,
        'avg_name_length': average length of contact names (float),
        'longest_name': the longest contact name
    }
    """
    # Your code here — use comprehensions to extract data
    return {}

def group_by_domain(contact_list):
    """Group contacts by email domain.
    Returns a dict like: {'example.com': ['Alice', 'Bob'], 'work.org': ['Charlie']}
    """
    # Your code here
    return {}

# Test it
stats = contact_stats(contacts)
print(f"Total: {stats['total']}")
print(f"Domains: {stats['domains']}")
print(f"Avg name length: {stats['avg_name_length']:.1f}")
print(f"Longest name: {stats['longest_name']}")

print("\nGrouped by domain:")
for domain, names in group_by_domain(contacts).items():
    print(f"  {domain}: {', '.join(names)}")
test_code:
test_contacts = [
    {'name': 'Alice', 'phone': '555-0001', 'email': 'alice@example.com'},
    {'name': 'Bob', 'phone': '555-0002', 'email': 'bob@example.com'},
    {'name': 'Charlie', 'phone': '555-0003', 'email': 'charlie@work.org'},
]
stats = contact_stats(test_contacts)
assert isinstance(stats, dict), 'contact_stats should return a dict'
assert stats['total'] == 3, f"total should be 3, got {stats['total']}"
assert stats['domains'] == 2, f"domains should be 2, got {stats['domains']}"
assert isinstance(stats['avg_name_length'], float), 'avg_name_length should be a float'
expected_avg = (5 + 3 + 7) / 3
assert abs(stats['avg_name_length'] - expected_avg) < 0.01, f"avg_name_length should be {expected_avg}, got {stats['avg_name_length']}"
assert stats['longest_name'] == 'Charlie', f"longest_name should be Charlie, got {stats['longest_name']}"
groups = group_by_domain(test_contacts)
assert isinstance(groups, dict), 'group_by_domain should return a dict'
assert len(groups) == 2, f'Should have 2 domains, got {len(groups)}'
assert 'example.com' in groups, 'Should have example.com domain'
assert sorted(groups['example.com']) == ['Alice', 'Bob'], f"example.com should have Alice and Bob, got {groups['example.com']}"
assert groups['work.org'] == ['Charlie'], f"work.org should have Charlie, got {groups['work.org']}"
empty_stats = contact_stats([])
assert empty_stats['total'] == 0, 'Empty list should have total 0'
acceptance_criteria: contact_stats() returns dict with total, domains, avg_name_length, longest_name
Handles empty contact list without errors
group_by_domain() groups contacts by the part after @ in email
Returns dict mapping domain -> list of names
hints: Extract domains with a set comprehension: {c['email'].split('@')[1] for c in contact_list}
Average: sum(len(c['name']) for c in lst) / len(lst) — but watch for empty lists!
Use max() with a key: max(contact_list, key=lambda c: len(c['name']))['name']
For group_by_domain: build a dict, loop contacts, extract domain, setdefault or check if key exists
